---
title: "Worksheet 10 Solution"
author: "Fred Azizi"
date: "2023-04-18"
output: pdf_document
---

1. The long term distance calls made by the employees of a company are normally distributed with a mean of $6.5$ minutes and a standard deviation of 2 minutes. 

> a. What is the probability that a call lasts less than 4 minutes? 

> b. What is the probability that a call lasts between 5 to 10 minutes. 

> c. What percentage of calls lasts more than 7 minutes?

> **\underline{Answer:}**

> We note that $T\sim$N$(\mu=6.5, \sigma=2)$. 

> (a) $P(T<4)= P\left(\frac{T-\mu}{\sigma}<\frac{4-6.5}{2}\right)=P(Z<-1.25)=0.1056$.

> (b) $P(5<T<10)= P(T<10)-P(T\leq 5)= P\left(\frac{T-\mu}{\sigma}<\frac{10-6.5}{2}\right)-P\left(\frac{T-\mu}{\sigma}<\frac{5-6.5}{2}\right)=P(Z<1.75)-P(Z<-0.75)=0.9599 - 0.2266=0.7333$.

> (c) $P(T>7)= 1-P \left(Z \leq \frac{7-6.5}{2}\right)=P(Z<0.25)=0.5987$.

\newpage

2. You want to see if you want to take a course with a Statistics prof. in the upcoming spring semester. Grades are important to you and because of that, you want to know the average score for his past classes. You can't find this data, so you directly contact him. Prof replied your email by saying class grades are Normally distributed with $\sigma=3$ and more than 40% of students received 90 or higher...You find the average for fun!

> Naturally, you want to run away from the course! But grades distribution is interesting so you decide to use you S351 knowledge. What is the mean of this distribution?

> **\underline{Answer:}**

> Let $X$ be grades of that course. We know that $X\sim$ N($\mu$, $\sigma$=3). We also know that $P(X\geq 90)=0.4$. Therefore, $P\left(\frac{X-\mu}{\sigma}\geq \frac{90-\mu}{\sigma=3}\right)=P\left(Z>\frac{90-\mu}{3}\right)=0.4$. We can also conclude that $P\left(Z \leq \frac{90-\mu}{3}\right)=1-0.4=0.6$. Using table, we figure out that:

$$\frac{90-\mu}{3}=0.255 \rightarrow \mu=90-0.255\times 3= 89.235$$


\newpage

3. The manager of a gas station has observed that the times required by drivers to fill their car's tank and
pay are quite variable. In fact, the times are exponentially distributed with a mean of 7.5 minutes. 

> a. What is the probability that a transaction is completed in less than 5 minutes?

> b. What is the probability that a car can complete the transaction between 5 minutes to 10 minutes?

> c. What is the standard deviation of time until a transaction is completed?

> **\underline{Answer:}**

> $\mu=7.5$. We know $\frac{1}{\lambda}=\mu$. Hence, $\lambda=\frac{1}{7.5}=0.13$. Therefore, $T\sim \text{exp}(\lambda=0.13)$.

> (a) Using formula in the textbook or slides: $P(T<5)=1-e^{-0.13 \times 5}=0.4779542$.

> (b)  Using formula in the textbook or slides: $P(5<T<10)=e^{-0.13 \times 5}-e^{-0.13 \times 10}=0.249514$.

> (c) For Exponential distribution, $\sigma=\mu=7.5$.

\newpage

4. Find the mean and standard deviations:

> a. $t_5$.

>> A: $E(t)=0$ regardless of degrees of freedom. $V(t)=\frac{5}{5-2}=\frac{5}{3}$. $Sd(t)=\sqrt{\frac{5}{3}}=1.291$.

> b. $\chi^2_9$.

>> A: $E\left(\chi^2_{9}\right)=9$. $V\left(\chi^2_9\right)=2\times 9=18$. $Sd\left(\chi^2_9\right)=\sqrt{18}=4.243$.

> c. $F_{5,15}$. 

>> A: $E(F_{5,15})=\frac{15}{15-2}=\frac{15}{13}$. $V(F_{5,15})=\frac{2 \times 15^2\left(5+15-2\right)}{5\left(15-2\right)^2\left(15-4\right)} \approx 0.8714363$. $Sd(F_{5,15})=\sqrt{V(F_{5,15})}=0.935$.

\newpage 

1. A sample of 40 retirees is drawn at random from a population whose mean age is 72 and standard deviation is 9. 

> a. What is the distribution of the sample mean? 

>> **\underline{Answer:}**

>> $\bar{X} \rightarrow$ mean age of retirees based on a sample of $n=40\rightarrow$ $\bar{X} \sim N(72 , \frac{9}{\sqrt{40}})=N(72,1.423)$

> b. What is the probability that the mean age of the sample exceeds 73 years old?

>> **\underline{Answer:}**


\begin{align}
P(\bar{X}> 73)&= 1- P(\bar{X}\leq 73) \notag\\
&= 1- P(\frac{\bar{X}-72}{1.423}\leq \frac{73-72}{1.423}) \tag{Standardize to use table}\\
&=1-P(Z \leq 0.70) \tag{From table, $P(Z<0.70)=0.758$}\\
&=1-0.7580=0.242 \notag
\end{align}


> c. What is the probability that the mean age of the sample is at most 73 years old?

>> **\underline{Answer:}**

>>Using part b:

$$P(\bar{X}\leq73)=1-P(\bar{X}>73)=0.7580$$ 

> d. What is the probability that the mean age of the sample is between 72 and 75 years old?

>> **\underline{Answer:}**

\begin{align}
P(72<\bar{X}<75) &=P(\bar{X}<75)-P(\bar{X}\leq 72)\notag\\
&=P(\frac{\bar{X}-72}{1.423}<\frac{75-72}{1.423})-P(\frac{\bar{X}-72}{1.423}\leq \frac{72-72}{1.423})\notag\\
&=P(Z<2.11)-P(Z<\leq 0) \notag \\
&=0.9826-0.5=0.4826 \notag
\end{align}


> e. What is the probability that a randomly selected retiree is over 73 years old?

>> **\underline{Answer:}**
 
>> This question is asking for $P(X>73)$. We can't answer this question since we don't know the distribution of the population. We only know the distribution of $\bar X$, not $X$ itself (later, we will learn how we may be able to estimiate this from data).  

\newpage

2. The amount of time spent by American adults playing Poker is Normally distributed with a mean of 4 hours and standard deviation of $1.25$ hours.

> a. Find the probability that a randomly selected American adult plays Poker for more than 5 hours per week. 

>> **\underline{Answer:}**

>> If we define $X$ as the amount of time spent by American adults playing Poker, then $X \sim N\left(\mu=4, \sigma=1.25\right)$.

$$
P(X>5)=1-P(X\leq 5)=1-P(Z\leq 0.8)=1-0.7881=0.2119
$$

> b. If four American adults are randomly selected, find the probability that: 

>> i. their average number of hours spent playing Poker is more than  5  hours per week. 

>>> **\underline{Answer:}**

>>> We know that $\bar{X} \sim N\left(4, \frac{1 \cdot 25}{\sqrt{4}}\right)$. Hence:

\begin{align}
P(\bar{X}>5)&=1-P[\bar{X}\leq 5]\notag\\
&=1-P\left(\frac{\bar{X}-4}{0.625} \leq \frac{5-4}{0.625}\right) \notag\\
&=1-P[Z<1.6]=1-0.9452=0.0548 \notag
\end{align}

>> ii. their average number of hours spent playing Poker is between 3 and 6 hours per week.

>>> **\underline{Answer:}**

\begin{align}
P(3<\bar{X}<6)&=P(\bar{X}<6)-P(\bar{X}\leq 3)\notag\\
&=P(Z<3.2)-P(Z<-1.6)\notag\\
&= 0.9993 - 0.0548=0.9445 \notag
\end{align}

>> iii. all four play Poker for more than 5 hours per week.

>>> **\underline{Answer:}**

$$
P(x>5) \cdot P(x>5) \cdot P(x>5) \cdot P(x>5)=\{P(x>5)\}^{4}=(0.2119)^{4}=0.002 
$$
\newpage
